title: St. Elizabeth, St. Hippolytus, St. Belane of the East & St. Fausta of Cyzicus
narrative:
title": "St. Elizabeth, St. Hippolytus, St. Belane of the East & St. Fausta of Cyzicus

narrative": "title\": \"St. Elizabeth, St. Hippolytus, St. Belane of the East & St. Fausta of Cyzicus

narrative\": \"St. Elizabeth the Righteous, St. Hippolytus of Rome, St. Belane of the East, and St. Fausta of Cyzicus

On this sixth day of Yekatit, the holy Church sets before the faithful a company of witnesses drawn from the hills of Judea, the city of Rome, the distant East, and the shores of the Propontis — four lives that together span the full geography and social range of the early Church. Saint Elizabeth, the righteous wife of the priest Zechariah and the mother of the Forerunner, stands at the threshold of the New Covenant as the first human voice to proclaim it aloud. When the Theotokos came to visit her in the hill country, Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit and cried out with a loud voice: Blessed are you among women, and blessed is the fruit of your womb! She is thus the first preacher of the Incarnation — not a bishop, not an apostle, not a scribe, but an elderly pregnant woman in the hills of Judea whose tongue the Spirit seized for the first Christological proclamation. The Ethiopian Church honours her as the prophetess of the Visitation.

Saint Hippolytus of Rome is a figure of extraordinary complexity and intellectual vitality: a theologian of such range and independence that he found himself at odds with successive bishops of Rome, eventually becoming an anti-pope in the Roman community, yet reconciled at the end and martyred alongside the very bishop he had opposed. His writings span the entire field of early Christian theology — scriptural commentary, liturgical regulation, anti-heretical polemic, and the famous Apostolic Tradition which preserves the earliest known text of a eucharistic prayer. The Ethiopian Church receives him not as a controversialist but as a doctor, a man whose restless intellect served ultimately the same faith that Elizabeth proclaimed in the hills of Judea, even when it led him into painful conflict with his brethren.

Saint Belane of the East is one of those figures who survive in the Synaxarium as a name and a testimony rather than a biography — a confessor from the eastern reaches of the Christian world whose faith brought her to martyrdom and whose story the tradition preserves in the spare, luminous style of the earliest martyrologies. She is sometimes identified with regions evangelised by the apostle Thomas or Thaddaeus, and her commemoration in the Ethiopian Synaxarium is a reminder that the Church spread eastward as vigorously as it spread westward, and that women were among its most intrepid missionaries and its most steadfast martyrs. Her name survives when countless others have not, and the tradition's preservation of it is itself an act of resurrection.

Saint Fausta of Cyzicus was a young Christian woman of that city on the Propontis who was brought before a magistrate named Evilasius and subjected to prolonged torture designed to break her confession. The tradition records that the very instruments of torture were turned to miraculous account: each attempt to destroy her faith instead produced in her a more luminous witness, and in the end Evilasius himself, watching her endurance, was converted by what he saw and confessed Christ alongside her, so that the two were martyred together. The Ethiopian Synaxarium preserves this story as a paradigm of the martyrological tradition's deepest insight: that the witness of the martyrs is not a performance of individual heroism but a contagion of grace that spreads from the condemned to the executioner, from the prisoner to the judge, from the weak to the powerful. In Fausta's suffering, Evilasius encountered the risen Lord. Amen.
